data.title: Americas Gold and Silver: An Undervalued Mid-Tier Producer
data.description:
Introduction

In my opinion, we are getting very close to breaking out in both silver and the silver miners. The HUI has been stuck in a trading range channel since 2013 (see below).

Once silver gets above $35, we should break out of this channel. Gold has been leading, but silver and the miners have lagged. In fact, the margins for silver miners have been awful. We need at least 25% FCF (free cash flow) margins for the silver miners to finally get a bid.

Hecla reported their earnings two days ago, and its share price got smashed down more than 10%. Why did they miss? Poor margins. What’s crazy about silver (and gold) miners is that they don’t have any competition or customers. Instead, they have buyers (plenty of them). This is both a good thing and a bad thing. The bad thing is that they are at the mercy of the silver price. The going spot price of the silver price dictates their margins.

Ironically, if silver was $2 higher last Qtr, then Hecla’s share price would be around $8. As speculators, we can bet that will be the case in the future. And if we are right, then companies like Americas Gold and Silver are going to rip higher from better margins.

Historically, Americas Gold and Silver (NYSE:USAS) (TSX:USA:CA) has been a poor performer and poor executor of hitting targets, and for this reason, many of you are wondering why I’m writing it up. Well, guess what? They recently got a new CEO. He came from Karora and did a great job there. Karora was acquired by Westgold, and the CEO had to find a new job. As I like to say, focus on the future and not the past.

I was a fan of Americas Gold and Silver before the new CEO arrived because of Galena. There are not very many quality silver mines in the world, and Galena is one of them. It’s in an excellent location in Idaho, has high grade, and strong exploration potential. Galena alone is worth 3x their market cap. Why is it so cheap? It’s history. Investors avoid problems.

Stock Name

Symbols

Type

Category

Share Price (US)

FD Shares

FD Mkt Cap (2/15/2025)

Americas Gold and Silver

USAS

Silver

Mid-Tier Producer

54 cents

657M

$356M

Company Overview

Americas Gold and Silver was a mid-tier gold/silver producer in Nevada (Relief Canyon), Mexico (Cosala), and Idaho (Galena). However, they had to put their gold mine (Relief Canyon) on C&M (care & maintenance) because of high costs, and their share price crashed. Today, they are a turnaround story, but a good one.

In 2024, they re-acquired 100% of their flagship Galena silver mine in Idaho. It has a 170M oz silver resource at 400 gpt. Galena should produce 2M oz in 2025 and increase that total for the next few years. I'm expecting the AISC to be around $20, but it will probably be higher. The good news is that costs at Galena are expected to decrease as they increase production. Their Cosala silver property in Mexico should produce about 1 million oz of silver in 2025, with an AISC of around $20 from the offsets. It’s supposed to be a low-cost mine, but they lost money in 2023, and have been trying to get costs lower.

Cosala has a funded EC120 project. This project will use the Cosala mill to add 2.5M oz of AG production for 5 years, with an AISC of $11. Production is scheduled to begin in Q3 2025. This means that in 2026, they could be producing 5M oz of AG.

Note that Cosala has had road blockage problems in the past from protestors, and hopefully, that will not return. But it does add risk.

They have large resources, with about 225 million oz of silver, plus significant base metals (zinc and lead). This should allow them to grow silver production. However, at this time they are not giving guidance for production growth. I expect to see some guidance this year or next.

Their company presentation does not go into detail about the problems at Relief Canyon or when they will be fixed. So, it appears to be serious. Perhaps at $3,000 gold, it will be profitable. It is supposed to produce 70,000 oz a year (their share). The resources are not large (around 700,000 oz), but it has exploration potential on a large property. My guess is that it will be restarted at around 50K oz a year at a $1,500 AISC. They don't have a great balance sheet with about $2 million in cash and about $40 million in debt (a red flag). In 2016, they did a 12 to 1 reverse split. That was painful. Many investors avoid this stock because of its past problems.

Hopefully, all of their bad news is behind them. They lost money in 2022 due to Relief Canyon and again in 2023 from high costs at Cosala and Galena. Their FD market cap crashed from $400 million to $50 million. Ouch. It’s now back to $350 million, but there was a lot of share dilution since 2023.If you don't mind their historical baggage, they could be a highflyer if silver prices rise. They have huge leverage to silver prices. Plus, they might restart Relief Canyon at some point. Also, Galena is likely to increase production significantly with its large resources.

Company Info

Cash: $2M

Debt: $42M

Current Silver Resources: 200M oz AG at 300 gpt.

Estimated Future Silver Resources: 150M oz AG at 300 gpt.

Estimated Future Silver Production: 6M oz AG.

Estimated Future Silver All-in Costs (breakeven): $28 per oz.

Current Gold Resources: 700K oz AU at .7 gpt.

Estimated Future Gold Resources: 700K oz AU at .7 gpt.

Estimated Future Gold Production: 5K oz AU.

Estimated Future Gold All-in Costs (breakeven): $2,000 per oz.

Note: My future timeline is 3-4 years.

Scorecard (1 to 10)

Properties/Projects: 8

Costs/Grade/Economics: 7.5

People/Management: 7.5

Cash/Debt: 6.5

Location Risk: 8

Risk-Reward: 7.5

Upside Potential: 8

Production Growth Potential/Exploration: 8

Overall Rating: 7.5

Notes: The scale is 1 to 10, with 10 being best. This is just a general overview of the company, looking for strengths and weaknesses.

8.5: Excellent.

8: Very Good.

7.5: Almost Good.

7: Satisfactory.

6.5: Marginal.

6: Weak.

Strengths/Positives

Significant upside potential.

Strong production growth potential.

Good mine location (mining district).

Strong management.

Strong insiders.

High leverage to higher silver prices.

Exploration potential.

Note: These are my opinion and a general overview of their strengths/weaknesses.

Risks/Red Flags

Likely some share dilution.

Potential lower gold/silver prices.

Weak balance sheet.

Speculation stock (unexpected events).

Assuming higher gold/silver prices.

Long-term investment is needed for them to grow production.

Note: These are my opinion and a general overview of their risks.

Valuation ($4,000 gold prices and $80 silver prices)

Silver production estimate for the long term: 6M oz.

All-In Costs (break-even): $28 per oz.

6M oz. x ($80 - $28) = $312 million annual FCF.

Gold production estimate for the long term: 50K oz.

All-In Costs (break-even): $2,000 per oz.

50K oz. x ($4,000 - $2,000) = $100 million annual FCF.

Total FCF = $312 + $100 = $432 million.

$432 million x 15 (multiplier) = $6.5 billion.

Current FD market cap: $356 million.

Upside potential: 1,700%.

Note: This valuation is for 2028 and assumes they can reach 6M oz AG production at $28 per/oz break-even costs per oz (FCF). Plus, 50K oz AU at $2,000 per/oz break-even costs.

Note: I used $4,000 gold prices to identify their future value because I am a long-term investor who plans to wait for higher gold prices. While $4,000 might seem too high as a future price, I expect gold to reach $3,000 in 2025. If we use a GSR (gold-silver ratio) of 50, then we get $80 silver price. The GSR was 39 in 2011, so a 50 seems conservative.

Note: My All-In Costs are the expected costs that will generate FCF.

Note: I used a future FCF multiplier of 15 because I do not expect significant share dilution. I could have used a lower multiple to account for dilution and risk. Because we are projecting out 3 years into the future, this estimate is only a best guess.

Balance Sheet/Share Dilution

We can expect some share dilution because of their weak balance sheet, which has very little cash. Plus, they may need to raise money to restart Relief Canyon in Nevada, or expand production at Galena. I’m hoping they can borrow what they need.

Risk/Reward

The risk-reward is very good, in my opinion, because of all the Galena mine, which has a lot of high grade silver. Other than the balance sheet risk and their current low margins, they seem well positioned to benefit from higher silver prices.

In addition to owning a large, long-life, high-grade silver mine, the other thing that makes this company appealing is the location of the Galena mine. It’s in Idaho. There are very few large silver mines in safe locations now that Mexico has become no longer reliable as a mining-friendly country.

Investment Thesis

(Note: The section can be redundant in some of my articles because I tend to repeat some ideas).

I always say that you can't pick winners, and that all you can do is pick potential winners. You never know which of your miners are going to do well. I've learned that from experience. The ones that do well tend to surprise you.

Americas Gold and Silver is a strong potential winner because of Galena, potential high FCF at higher silver prices, exploration potential, good location, strong insiders, a good management team, and a current low valuation.

I think they could easily generate more FCF than their current FD mkt cap. This won’t happen in 2025 or 2026, but potentially in 2027 or 2028. This is a long-term bet where we give them time to expand production at Galena and restart Relief Canyon.

Final Thoughts

If you are a believer like I am that gold is going to $4,000 (pushing silver above $75), then Americas Gold and Silver is a pretty good bet. These are the types of high-alpha stocks that I look for. I like to find undervalued producer that are expecting to grow production and lower their costs.

I also like the management team, with its new proven CEO, which, I think, can expand production significantly and execute much better than the previous team.

The key to the upside is going to be gold and silver prices. Also, we don’t want them to dilute shares severely. Plus, we want them to expand production significantly.

I don’t know what’s going to happen to the valuation of silver producers for the rest of this decade if silver prices explode, but my guess is that they will be a lot more valuable than the current valuations. My guess is that FCF multiples are going to explode higher. I don’t think it’s crazy to think that Americas Gold and Silver could reach a 20.

I always look for 5-baggers with 10-bagger potential. Americas Gold and Silver fits that thesis. However, don’t expect it to be a slam dunk. Never over-allocate to a stock because you like it a lot and think it is a slam dunk. However, this is probably one you don’t want to under-allocate. Best guess!

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners: Rating Downgrade On High Valuation And Visible Headwinds
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a buy rating for Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (NASDAQ:CCEP) when I wrote about it last year in March, as I expected a positive growth outlook for FY24, especially with the inclusion of the Philippines business unit. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I recommend a hold rating. My key update is that while CCEP continues to execute well, the ongoing volume decline in Western Europe and uncertainties in Indonesia make it difficult to justify further upside. Additionally, with the stock trading at a valuation premium to peers, I believe the risk-reward is now less attractive.

Review

CCEP reported earnings on 14th Feb, with revenue coming in at EUR 20.7 billion, a 3.5% y/y growth on an FX-neutral basis. Revenue performance was slightly below consensus expectations, primarily due to continued volume weakness in Western Europe. However, it was good to see that CCEP was able to drive a 2.6% y/y increase in revenue per unit case. Comparable EBIT grew 8% y/y, helped by cost-saving initiatives that offset cost inflation. In terms of free cash flow, CCEP generated EUR 1.8 billion in free cash flow [FCF] for FY24, which is amazing considering that CCEP invested EUR 1 billion back into the business. For FY25, management reaffirmed its outlook, which includes FX-neutral revenue growth of ~4% and EBIT growth of ~7%.

I walked away from CCEP earnings with a balanced view compared to my bullish view previously. On the positive side of things, firstly, I really like CCEP’s execution in growing pricing without significant volume loss. Take 4Q24, for instance; despite a rather difficult consumer spending backdrop (which caused negative volume growth in 4Q24), revenue per unit case still grew 2.6%. This shows that CCEP’s revenue growth management [RGM] – price-pack architecture, promotions, product mix management, etc. – is working.

Secondly, the Philippines continues to be an attractive growth region, which CCEP saw double-digit volume growth in FY24. Notably, CCEP gained ~300 bps of value share in sparkling and now holds about 50% of the NARTD (non-alcohol ready to drink) category. If you recall my 2Q23 earnings update for CCEP, I pointed out that this region is a fast-growing market with a large total addressable market. With CCEP owning a sizeable market share, I remain confident in CCEP's ability to tap into this market’s long-term growth potential—driven by a young, growing population and increasing per capita consumption. It was also great to hear management stepping on the acceleration pedal as they accelerate capital investments, pulling forward new production lines and distribution infrastructure. This investment should help sustain high single-digit growth in the Philippines and offset some of the weaker volume trends in Western Europe.

Lastly, there is a good chance that CCEP gets included in the FTSE 100, which could provide a near-term technical catalyst, as passive funds tracking the index may increase their exposure to the stock. While this does not change CCEP’s fundamentals, it could support share price performance in the near term.

On the negative side of things, my major concern is that volume growth in Western Europe has not meaningfully recovered. Away-from-home demand declined 3.9% y/y in 4Q24, and while CCEP has managed to offset this through pricing, the trend of volume decline is still a headwind. Both away-from-home and at-home volumes were also down at 0.9% and 1.1% y/y, respectively. Again, while pricing has offset this decline, there is only so much that CCEP can raise prices, especially given the poor consumer spending environment in Europe.

Likewise, in Indonesia, the outlook is uncertain. It was so bad that CCEP had to take an EUR 175 million impairment charge related to its Indonesian operations, which is evidence that underlying demand is weaker than expected. While management remains committed to the market, there is little that management can do to resolve geopolitical factors (boycott of certain brands). Granted that this could be temporary issues and that CCEP continues to invest in improving its route-to-market strategy, the timing of a full recovery remains uncertain.

Valuation limits upside potential

Lastly, now that CCEP trades at ~19x forward PE (was 16x when I wrote in March last year), which is near its 10-year high, the upside seems limited as well. When compared to other non-alcoholic beverage players, CCEP actually seems overvalued for its growth and margin profile. The industry is expected to grow at mid-single digits for the next two years with an average net margin of ~9%; this compares similarly to CCEP, but CCEP trades at ~4x the premium to the industry. I don’t think this premium is justified, especially given the visible headwinds I noted above. But I do recognize that the technical catalyst may be supporting the share price here, which has nothing to do with CCEP’s fundamentals. As such, I am only downgrading to hold and not a sell.

Final thoughts

My recommendation is a hold. CCEP remains a well-managed company with strong execution and an attractive long-term opportunity in the Philippines. However, I have concerns about the weak volume trends in Europe and the geopolitical headwinds in Indonesia. CCEP’s valuation also appears stretched relative to peers, limiting upside. While a potential FTSE 100 inclusion could provide a short-term technical boost, I prefer to wait for more clarity on volume recovery before turning bullish again.

data.title: Apple: Has One Of The Biggest Opportunities In The World Ahead Of It
data.description:
The Apple Investment Thesis

After Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) relentlessly outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) over the past 10 years, as we can see in the chart above, I expect the next 10 years to be similar or even better. Because Apple has one of the biggest opportunities in front of it, simply because it has an incredible number of Apple devices in circulation that can be leveraged as the health ecosystem matures. In fact, I think the health ecosystem could be as successful for Apple as the cloud was and is for Amazon (AMZN).

Apple Health and Apple Health Monitoring

Apple has one of the greatest opportunities of our time on its hands. The large number of Apple ecosystem devices in circulation gives Apple a hard-to-combat competitive advantage. The 2.2 billion Apple devices currently in use are simply something that has to be earned over years of high-quality products, services, and an ecosystem that works almost without interruption. And if Apple can capitalize on this huge share of devices with health monitoring and life-enhancing services, it will be an even bigger success than the iPhone.

On the one hand, this will increase the service share of sales, which is the high-margin business, which will have a positive effect on earnings, and on the other hand, Apple already has the perfect customer thanks to its premium positioning. Apple customers have no problem spending money, as evidenced by the ever-increasing prices of iPhones, and affluent customers tend to be more willing to invest in their health. These are ideal conditions for Apple.

In addition, Apple's design department is skilled at creating products that are trendy and timely. I mean, how many younger people who have hearing problems don't wear hearing aids because they don't think it's cool. But now you can wear AirPods Pro 2 with hearing aid functionality and no one would know you were using them for that functionality because the product is already so socially embedded.

And I think in the future we'll see other things that are picked up by our iPhones, AirPods, or Apple watches that will give us real-time data that we can either have doctors or AI analyze. For example, I would not be surprised if in 5 years Apple devices are more powerful in some areas of monitoring than many second-tier hospitals around the world are today.

Apple is known for making its products as user-friendly and intuitive as possible, and that is where I think many medical device companies fail today. Anyone who has ever had one of those clunky and expensive sleep monitors at home probably knows what I mean. And with Apple's cash position and excellent employees, I could see Apple disrupting many medical areas, either through its own R&D or through M&A, and then improving the design of the components.

Butterfly iQ, for example, is a mobile phone-based ultrasound solution that is already being used in many practices to replace old, bulky equipment. It is quicker to set up and can even be used outside the office in an emergency. A win-win for patients and medical staff. And I fully expect that Apple's ecosystem will continue to expand to include many medical applications and devices that will make life easier and safer for many people.

And most importantly, Apple is committed to protecting privacy to the fullest extent possible, so customers can rest assured that their sensitive health information is safe.

Apple's Programming Kits

With APIs such as HealthKit, ResearchKit, CareKit and SensorKit, Apple makes it easy for companies to place their apps in the Apple ecosystem and benefit from Apple's cutting-edge technology. The result is applications that provide significant value, such as StrivePD, which collects data through Apple's Movement Disorder API to provide the best care for Parkinson's patients.

In other words, the better the sensors, the better the apps, which in turn means that customers are more and more integrated into the Apple ecosystem, which drives up the cost of switching.

Apple's latest revenue figures

Product figures were roughly flat year-over-year, which is not too bad because first, the amount of equipment in circulation is already massive, and second, the service business has much better margins anyway.

And if we look at the gross margins, we see that Products is about 39%, while Services is almost twice as high at 75%. And since the Services segment is growing much faster, the gross margin should continue to grow.

The iPhone product line generated lower revenue in the quarter compared to the year-ago quarter, partially offset by higher Mac and iPad revenue. Much more important, however, is the fact that service revenues continue to grow at a double-digit rate (~14%). Because as long as they grow faster than the product category, this leads to better margins in the long term. And because I think Apple is going to be one of the major players in the health services space, I think Apple is going to see a lot of margin expansion going forward.

Therefore, I do not consider the declining sales in the Chinese market, which have caused some concern, to be that important for the time being. This is because they will increase again as soon as Apple gets going in the health sector. As I do not believe that the combination of the devices in circulation and Apple's design and usability expertise can be beaten.

Apple's ROIC + Capital Allocation

In addition, Apple is one of the few companies that has been able to improve its already astonishingly high ROIC. For example, the low for the last 5 years was 28%, which would have been a fantastic result for over 95% of companies worldwide, but even that has been beaten and ROIC is now 56% with a 5-year average of 49%. Therefore, it should come as no surprise that the WACC is significantly beaten and that Apple has a strongly positive ROIC-WACC spread.

  • ROIC: 56.16%
  • WACC: 8.4%
  • ROIC-WACC Spread: 56.16% - 8.4% = 47.76%

And in addition to its fantastic ROIC, Apple is also very shareholder-friendly, as evidenced by its steadily declining share count and steadily increasing dividend. This, coupled with increasing revenues, is a recipe for great total shareholder returns.

Apple's Balance Sheet

The fact that Apple also has a great balance sheet is the icing on the cake. In fact, the $53 billion in cash and cash equivalents exceeds the $10 billion in current portion of LT debt by a multiple.

And Apple's times earned interest is 3732x, while the average company in the S&P 500 has only about 10x interest coverage. So the incredible quality of Apple is not just in its capital allocation and return on capital, but also in its interest coverage and balance sheet. In fact, interest coverage or times interest earned clearly shows that Apple's liquidity and financial strength are much better than the financial health of the average company. So Apple is exceptionally well positioned, and its balance sheet shows no major weaknesses in terms of future liquidity or strength.

With not much debt coming due in the next few years, as we can see in the chart above, the cash and cash equivalents can be used for investments. And here I expect that this cash will be used for M&A activities in the healthcare sector as interesting opportunities arise.

If we look at Apple's M&A history, we see that in recent years there have been 2-3 transactions per year. In 2024, these were DarwinAI in the AI sector and Pixelmator, a software for digital art and photography. In health, Apple acquired Tueo Health, an asthma monitoring company, in 2018, and Beddit, a sleep monitoring hardware company, in 2017, and is using them on the Apple Watch and iOS.

That's why I think we're going to see about 2 to 3 company acquisitions again in 2025, and I think the focus will be on AI and health. With many AI companies also likely to bring further advancements to the Apple Health ecosystem through their models.

Risks

I mean, the obvious risks are that Apple will not be the winner in healthcare and that other companies will manage to attack Apple's moat. Because If Apple is no longer seen as a status symbol, which, I think, is unrealistic at the moment, then sales would definitely take a hit. But I still often see people look at you with disdain if you don't have an iPhone, so I think that moat is still in place.

Other smartphone manufacturers have a similar number of devices in use and have also built an ecosystem, like Samsung, for example. But Apple still has the advantage that its customer base, in my opinion, is much less sensitive to price increases or high prices because Apple is perceived as a premium manufacturer. So Apple will probably be able to charge a premium for health applications.

I mean, if Apple can capture just 10% of the current 2.2 billion Apple devices and charge them $9 a month, that would be 220 million x $9 = $1.98 billion in additional revenue each month, or just under $24 billion a year in additional high-margin revenue. And this is just a small part of the potential I see. In fact, I think there will be a number of applications and devices that will make the ecosystem something huge.

Valuation

Apple is currently trading at 38x, which is above the 5-year average of 29x. But I think this is justified because, first, Apple is likely to benefit from margin expansion as the share of services grows. Second, Apple has stayed out of the AI arms race and will likely be more cost-efficient than many of its peers, and third, Apple is simply a better company today than it was five years ago. And Apple simply has incredible opportunities that justify a premium to the 5-year average.

If we take a look at the projected cap ex spending, we can see that the megacaps plan to spend more than $300 billion in 2025. Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft, and Amazon alone plan to make $320 billion in data center and AI CapEx investments, while Apple's strategy currently looks more like renting capacity from cloud providers than investing heavily in infrastructure itself. That is why I am assuming at the moment that Apple will be more cost-efficient in the field of AI.

Based on the fact that there are more Apple devices in circulation today than there were five years ago, and that the iPhones, the Apple Watches, and the ecosystem have all evolved, I think Apple is stronger than it was five years ago. The bigger the ecosystem, the more customers are locked in. As a result, the switching costs are also high.

And since I believe that the healthcare ecosystem will start to generate revenue growth in the next few years, I think the analyst estimates above are too low. Especially given the fact that service revenue has much higher margins. I think AI will bring a lot of interesting apps and applications to the App Store and the ecosystem that will be high margin for Apple.

Assuming that the multiple in 5 years is the same as today, therefore 38x, the analyst estimates for FY29 EPS of $13.96 would result in a share price of ~$530. This alone would represent an upside of more than 100% from today's share price of ~$244.

However, if we compare the actual EPS to the analyst estimates, we see that there has been a beat every time since 2013, and the analysts have underestimated Apple's strength. And I think that will continue. Therefore, I think it is very likely that FY29 EPS will be above estimates and EPS in the $15+ range is realistic.

Conclusion

The opportunity presented by the 2.2 billion devices in circulation is simply gigantic. If Apple can build an ecosystem in health monitoring or health that is as high quality as the current one, I think my estimate is very conservative. Because then Apple can probably win much more than 10% of the devices for this product. And then there are the numerous up-selling and cross-selling opportunities. So the potential is just huge. And that is why I see a lot of potential for Apple in the long term.

data.title: Lower Levered Ares Capital Still Generates More Than Enough
data.description:
With each report, Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) demonstrated an ability to outperform its payouts. Once again, the February report for December proved that thesis true. Management continues with a constant dividend in spite of stellar earnings. With this business development company (BDC), two performance factors drive current and future performances, leverage and investment rates of return. With Ares, the rate of return highly correlates with Fed overnight interest rates. The two track each other. With a neutral or accommodating stance now in vogue, the question that investors ask, can earnings continue high enough to pay the long-time $0.48 per quarter dividend? Even more important, will management ever decide to increase the dividend? When is enough? Well, we continue our coverage, the last Predicting Ares Capital's Performance predicted results at a 3.5% Fed Interest Rate target. We outlined puts and takes. That quest continues.

4th Quarter

We begin with the quarter results summaries in the next slide.

A few other critical results follow: core earnings and Net Investment Income each equaled $0.55 while leverage trended flat at approximately 1.00 net cash considerably lower than the target of 1.20.

Of other interest, LTM EBITDA of portfolio companies grew 11% up from 10% in the 3rd quarter and 9% in the final quarter of 23. We believe that this comment by management reflects and conveys an important practice of the company.

"In 2024, over 70% of our new commitments were to existing borrowers, and importantly, we are increasingly being asked to provide a larger portion of our borrowers' overall capital structures. As an illustration, our share of the overall financings for our top 10 largest incumbent borrowers more than doubled in each of the past three quarters."

Non-accrual rates ticked up slightly to 1.7% up 40 basis points yet, still significantly under long-term averages of 2.8%.

Management noted that the lower earnings came from, "Our decline in core earnings was largely driven by the impact of the decline in yields from the portfolio as base rates at the end of the year were nearly 100 basis points lower than where they were at the end of 2023."

In terms of our leverage, the company ended the fourth quarter with debt-to-equity ratio, net of available cash, of 0.99 times, down from the 1.03 a quarter ago. Management added, "We believe our significant amount of dry powder positions us well to continue supporting our existing portfolio company commitments as well as new investing activities."

History Table of Key Performance

Our factors table from our last article is listed next slightly modified follows.

Key Performance

Factors

4th

24

3rd

24

2nd

24

1st

24

4th

23

3rd

23
Core $0.55 $0.58 $0.61 $0.59 $0.63 $0.59
NI $0.55 $0.57 $0.58 $0.55 $0.60 $0.52
Leverage 1.03 1.06 1.06 0.99 1.07 1.07
Fed Rates 4.3 $4.75 -4.6% 5.5 5.5 5.5 5.5
Weighted Average Yield 10% 10.7% 11.3% 11.2%

Of most importance for investors, the Weighted Average Yield fail approximately 1.2% while the Fed rates dropped roughly the same.

Marketplace

Management discussed the marketplace experience for 2024 labeling it strong. With the low leverage, they noted the ability to put a lot of capital to work going into the future. Then, they added this interesting statistic,

"During 2024, we reviewed a record volume of new opportunities totaling more than $650 billion, and the number of potential investments we evaluated during the year grew each quarter on a year-over-year basis. Given the strength of our pipeline, we were also able to generate a record level of new commitments, net of repayments, which totaled $5 billion for the year."

With its largest publicly traded BDC, management claimed an advantage in a very competitive market.

They reminded the analysts that they have the ability to compete in the lower, middle and upper middle marketplace. Within the lower and middle markets, deals carry a 25 to 50 basis point advantage.

When asked about spreads going forward by Mark Hughes of Truist Securities, Kipp deVeer, CEO, opined:

"I think most of the decrease we saw pretty ratably through last year. 100, 150 basis points has been the number that we quoted elsewhere in terms of the declines that we've seen, whether it was repricing existing names or new deals. For the time being, I feel like it's pretty much plateaued again because we play across the entire spectrum, smaller companies, larger companies."

Ares is seeing the marketplace stabilize in the past few months.

Dividend

Next, a subject of the most importance, the dividend, deserves a discussion. When asked, DeVeer, noted that they had had a long discussion about a special and for many reasons decided against one. He was very coy with his answer, no detail. He added that this has to wait until next year.

Included within this subject was discussion about lifting leverage. He stated,

"Yeah, I think we'd like the leverage ratio to be higher. Being able to increase the leverage ratio is obviously a driver of earnings, which I think will be important if and when rates continue to kind of come down, right? That's one of the countervailing levers that we can pull to drive earnings in the face of tighter spreads and lower rates. So, we're fortunate in that we're still materially outearning the core dividend so we don't feel a desperate need to do that. But again, just a reminder, that's a lever that we can and will pull."

For the first time in our coverage, management admitted that this is a critical parameter for increasing yields with or when rates fall once again. Now, we have to ask, what do they see? Why isn't the leverage increasing? Is it possible that there aren't enough good opportunities to cover the need for this very large investment? They reviewed $600 billion opportunities and committed to $5 billion. We aren't sure. But this is the most important area to monitor. For now, they earn plenty to pay the dividend and more.

The company still carries forward a lot of undistributed income. From the 10-Q, "Ares Capital estimates that it will carry forward excess taxable income of approximately $922 million or $1.37."

Risk

BDCs face recession risks like any other investment. One might be coming. Ed Dodd, a former very successful data miner with Wall Street, wrote a column on the probability of a recession. Within his comments, he included at least two gems,

“We are seeing a recession in 2025. The rest of the globe is already starting to roll over. It’s going to be a worldwide recession. There is going to be a mini housing crisis."

And,

"So, we are going to have a recession . . . Then, Trump gets his policies, and he has a very short window of opportunity to get all of his policies enacted. If he does, we will be booming on the other side of this.”

For investors, Ares does heavily consider downside risks. From the call,

"As a sign of additional strength in our portfolio, at the end of the fourth quarter, our weighted average loan to value was 44%, which we believe provides us with strong downside protection . . ."

The story is certainly mixed. From commentary of the latest inflation report,

"January's hot inflation report coupled with the stellar monthly jobs report last week delivered a mighty blow to rate-cut expectations, with some on Wall Street reiterating calls for the Federal Reserve interest rates to remain on a long hold for the year."

With rates likely on hold, what seems clear, Ares Capital earns more than enough to pay the dividend. Whether to buy, sell or hold depends on stock price. Unless we see efforts to increase leverage, stock prices near $23 suggest a hold rating. Thus, we have a hold on the stock. We own a lot of shares and don't plan to sell anytime soon. We just believe that after the dividend is paid in six weeks, a cheaper price will come. The lower leverage posture won't in our view affect the dividend anytime soon but just don't get a mindset for pay raises anytime soon. And if a recession hits, the conservative posture will provide protection. We, also, must wonder, can Ares find enough solid investments for increasing leverage? To find $5 billion last year, management gleaned through $600 billion. For the needed increase, the gleaning now requires almost a trillion, yes, a trillion. But enough is enough.

data.title: H World Group: The Undisputed Value-For-Money Hospitality Leader With Global Ambitions
data.description:
We are bullish on H World Group (NASDAQ:HTHT) as we believe the company is well-positioned for several secular trends in China’s hospitality industry, with overseas expansion as an attractive call option for investors.

With the stock up 12% YTD, we believe there is still incremental upside. HTHT is a key beneficiary of China’s consumption downgrade trend, in which tourism has shifted towards more domestic travel rather than overseas destinations. The demand for maximizing value for money on affordable leisure directly benefits HTHT, given its focus on China’s mid-scale and economy hotel chains.

Besides the leisure travel segment, we also see HTHT benefit from China’s weak macro backdrop as businesses scale down their travel budget, which favors HTHT’s mid-scale hotel brands given their price range falls within the budget range.

We believe HTHT’s unique position within the economy and the mid-scale hospitality segment attracts both leisure and business travelers and is gradually transforming the company into an undisputed value-for-money hospitality brand with a growing network of over 250 million members.

In the near and medium term, lower-tier cities offer HTHT attractive growth potential, given that these regions position HTHT at the intersection of value-of-money consumption from both leisure and business travelers and travel premiumization, where travelers from lower-tier cities and towns are upgrading to HTHT brands from unbranded budget hotels.

In the long run, we believe HTHT is making the right strategic decision by pivoting to overseas with its 2020 acquisition of Deutsche Hospitality. This acquisition could serve as a springboard for HTHT to further its expansion in Europe and fast-growing regions such as the Greater Gulf and Southeast Asia, where there is a lack of reputable economy hotel brands.

We value HTHT with a target EV/EBITDA multiple of 16x based on the 2026E consensus estimate. The stock currently trades at 14.8x forward EBITDA compared with branded global peers such as Marriott and Hilton, which trade between 16x and 18x. Given HTHT's global growth potential, we believe it should trade at a premium compared with its domestic peers, such as Atour, which trades at 12.6x.

Beneficiary of China’s consumption downgrade trend

China’s weak macro backdrop has significantly altered consumer travel behavior, with a shift towards domestic tourism and a value-for-money travel experience rather than overseas travel. This trend uniquely positions H World as a key beneficiary as its economy and mid-scale hotel brands cater to the growing demand for affordable yet high-quality accommodations.

According to the latest Chinese New Year travel data, domestic tourist numbers during the CNY holiday reached 501 million (+5.9% y/y) and +25.8% vs. the 2019 level. While tourism revenue grew by 7.0% y/y to RMB 677 bn, per capita spending remains 8% below the 2019 level, implying that travelers remain budget-conscious and prefer cost-effective travel options.

The shift towards affordable leisure has been ongoing for some time as we have seen the rising popularity of destination hotspots driven by China’s Gen-Z population facing high unemployment rates and flexible lifestyles. The emergence of tourist hotspots such as Zibo BBQ and Tianshui hotpot is evidence that Chinese travelers are maximizing their travel budget domestically given the budget and travel constraints of visiting overseas. H World’s extensive network in these regions positions it well to capture this affordable travel demand, given its ability to provide consistent quality and service within the travelers' budget.

HTHT’s 3Q24 was characterized by further weakness in its ADR and RevPAR due to weak macro, but we believe that the strength in Q4 and CNY periods could see a slight improvement in RevPAR with 2025 RevPAR likely to remain flat vs. 2024

Management guided that 2025 growth is likely to be driven by hotel openings and margin improvement due to the hotel mix towards less leased and owned hotels as HTHT is shifting towards the asset-light model.

Given the high-quality hotel supply remains scarce in the market, HTHT’s focus on balancing cost, quality and efficiency is likely to allow the brand to remain competitive in the highly competitive budget hotel environment.

Corporate travelers migrating towards HTHT

The corporate travel segment is also seeking greater value for money, given the weak macro backdrop as corporations scale down travel budgets and the hotel budget for reimbursement.

In the latest 3Q2024, HTHT reported a 41% y/y increase in B2B bookings, driven by its efforts to strengthen direct sales capabilities and partnerships with corporate clients. The company’s focus on enhancing its membership program and offering exclusive benefits, such as the lowest pricing guarantees, has further solidified its appeal among business travelers. With over 4,500 active corporate clients, we believe HTHT is well-positioned to maintain its leadership in the mass-market corporate travel segment.

The rising penetration HTHT has been able to achieve within both leisure and corporate is driving its membership base to surpass 250m in the most recent Q. Close to 2/3 of the bookings were made through its H World CRS, transforming HTHT into a reputable brand within the budget and mid-scale hospitality segment similar to the global brands that are focused in the mid and the high-end segments.

Lower-tier cities driving near to medium-term growth

HTHT’s near-term growth strategy will focus on lower-tier city expansion, where the demand for affordable high-quality hospitality services is expanding.

As of last quarter, 42% of HTHT’s hotels were located in Tier 3 and below cities, with this mix expected to increase. The company’s pipeline includes 53% of hotels in lower-tier cities, and we see this strategy not only taps into the growing demand for value-for-money accommodations but also allows HTHT to establish a first-mover advantage in these regions by building its brand equity within the emerging traveler group.

The expansion into lower-tier cities is unique in that it sits at the intersection of consumption downgrade and premiumization trends. Budget travel has been driving the demand for economy and midscale hotels on the consumption downgrade side. On the premiumization trend, travelers who visit lower-tier cities from towns and smaller regions may prefer HTHT, given its superior quality and service relative to mom-and-pop hotels or unbranded options.

Long-term growth to be driven by global expansion

Although China remains HTHT’s largest revenue contributor at around 80% of total revenue, ex-China growth is becoming increasingly attractive after HTHT acquired DH in 2020, which sets the stage for its global growth potential. Europe is a growth market relative to China, but we feel that the GCC and ASEAN regions are particularly attractive due to their growing tourism industries and underpenetrated budget hotel markets, and this gap presents a significant growth opportunity for HTHT.

In 3Q2024, DH’s blended RevPAR grew by 3.7% y/y, driven by a 2.5% increase in ADR and a 0.8 percentage point rise in occupancy. The company’s restructuring efforts, including the optimization of its Zleep brand and the closure of underperforming leased hotels, are expected to enhance profitability and support future growth.

The valuation upside remains attractive

We feel that HTHT should be valued by incorporating its brand equity and global growth potential. We are bullish on emerging leaders out of China that can leverage their expertise to expand globally, hence our bullish view on PDD (see: PDD: Navigating Tariff Uncertainty, Driving Long-Term Growth Through Global Expansion) and Meituan (see: Meituan: Capturing The Lens Of Opportunity).

We believe HTHT could become a global budget and midscale hotel brand and should be valued similarly to other global hotel brands such as MAR and IHG.

At 16x EV/EBITDA multiple, HTHT would trade on par with MAR but at the low end of the global branded hotel companies. This would position HTHT at a premium compared with its China-centric peers, such as Atour and Jin Jiang, which trade at low double-digit multiples. At 16x forward EBITDA, HTHT would have an implied value of $47/share, or 30% upside from the current level.

Catalysts for the stock include the upcoming earnings release, in which the market will take the improvement in RevPAR along with the ongoing shareholder return policy positively.

The risks to our thesis are two-fold: First, while we are comfortable with the weak macro backdrop that drives consumers downgrading to HTHT’s hotels, we would be more concerned about consumers cutting back on travel altogether. The same concern applies to business travel, which is uncertain given China’s ongoing macro weakness. The other risk is that much of the growth in 2025 will likely come from hotel expansions and that the overall sector supply could slow down if the ongoing macro weakness continues.

data.title: Super Micro: Not Risk Free, But A Game Changer
data.description:
After a troubling period, Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) appears to have survived accounting allegations for a second time. The AI server company took some sales hits, but the business appears set to thrive again with U.S. hyperscalers full-speed ahead on AI data center spending. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock, especially on a breakout of resistance at $50 going back to August.

AI Rebound

Super Micro was booming heading into the end of 2H of last year when Hindenburg Research came out with a seething short report accusing the company of accounting issues. The company had already gone through similar accounting violations in the past, and the DoJ probe and 10-K delay caused the market to panic.

The short report accused the company with a lack of sufficient internal controls, alarming to the stock market and investors. Though, a lot of the allegations had to do with internal reporting and controls on related-party transactions, not so much technology related to direct-liquid cooling servers needed for the ongoing AI revolution or actual sales.

Our original theory supporting "sloppy accounting" and not fraud was the weak operating margins and volatile quarterly sales. Naturally, Super Micro would want to report higher margins and profits when undertaking an attempt to deceive investors, and the company eventually confirmed that no misconduct was found by an independent special committee.

The company isn't completely out of the woods on this issue. Super Micro had to replace the auditors, with BDO taking over and a target to release the FQ1 and FQ2 audited results on February 25 quickly approaching.

Super Micro just released preliminary FQ2 numbers, with revenue estimates of $5.6 to $5.7 billion. The business definitely took a hit with all of the accounting concerns, but the company maintained virtually all of the previous sales.

Elon Musk owned xAI was apparently a company shifting business from Super Micro. Most recently, Dell Technologies (DELL) is listed as potentially gaining a $5 billion server order from xAI after knowing Super Micro was already a huge supplier.

Regardless, Super Micro still guided FY25 revenues to $23.5 to $25.0 billion, down by $3.5 to $5.0 billion from the original target of $26.0 to $30.0 billion for the fiscal year. The company definitely took a sales hit in the short term, but the losses were far less than feared, considering the stock fell all the way to $17 in mid-November.

Super Micro was even able to raise $700 million via a convertible debt offering in an indication investors are more comfortable the AI server company doesn't have accounting issues impacting the business.

Big FY26 Guide

Considering Super Micro plunged so far a few months ago, the market thought the allegations could be near fatal for the business. Now, the company is guiding towards FY26 revenues of up to $40 billion.

Super Micro actually forecast a 60%+ rise in sales next fiscal year. The AI data center demand will definitely surge this year, with the major tech companies forecasting a massive boost in spending. In total, the big tech companies now plan to spend over $325 billion on capital expenditures in 2025.

The stock has already rallied back to $48, so the easy money has already been made. Super Micro will now have to deliver the projected growth in order to reward shareholders, though the stock previously topped $100 when far lower estimates were predicted.

With the disruption, the key could be the cash position, with Super Micro constantly needing access to capital to fund the massive expansion in sales. The company needs to buy the GPUs from Nvidia (NVDA) in order to build the servers.

Super Micro ended 2024 with cash of $1.4 billion and total debt of $1.9 billion. The company raised the $700 million last week and apparently ended January with a cash balance already above $2 billion, providing Super Micro with a solid balance sheet to support further growth.

The strong sales level provides another good indication that Super Micro has the liquid cooling technology needed by data center operators. The company suggests the liquid cooling market will reach 30% in the next year, and the shift to Blackwell servers provides an opportunity for tech leaders.

As the investment thesis has held for a while, Super Micro was cheap based on the big EPS boost ahead from returning gross margins back towards the higher end of the range of up to 17%. The combination of massive revenue growth and much higher margins will lead to significant EPS growth.

Super Micro guided to FY26 revenues reaching $40 billion. The company has the following basic financial models for FY26:

14% Gross Margin

  • Revenue - $40 billion
  • Gross profit - 14%
  • Operating expense - 4%
  • Taxes - 14.6%
  • Net income - $3.42 billion
  • EPS (653 million share) - $5.23

17% Gross Margin

  • Revenue - $40 billion
  • Gross margin - 17%
  • Operating expense - 4%
  • Taxes - 14.6%
  • Net income - $4.44 billion
  • EPS (653 million shares) - $6.80

The company reported a preliminary FQ2 gross margin of 11.9%, so Super Micro is close to the bottom end of the range despite the accounting concerns. The consensus analyst estimates currently sit at only $33.2 billion, producing an EPS of only $3.74.

The upside potential is substantial, especially considering how the CEO stressed the FY26 revenue guidance is very conservative. On the preliminary FQ225 earnings call, CEO Charles Liang made the following statement:

We have industry standard product line plus lots of [chip set] (ph) including some confidential product under development. And we have a customer engaged with us for those projects. So this year, even 10-K delay, we grew about 60%. Last year we grew 110%. So the coming year, fiscal 2026, at this moment, we believe at least, we will grow 65% at least. So that's, I believe, a very conservative estimation.

And the past in production capacity. I mean, USA now, our utilization rate only about 55%. Taiwan utilization rate only about 60%. Malaysia utilization rate still about 1% only. So there are lots of room to grow for us.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the stock is cheap based on the earnings potential on the growth opportunities ahead. The biggest risk is that Super Micro runs into an auditing issue and the stock gets delisted. Otherwise, analysts have to substantially hike EPS estimates, leading to the stock likely trading below 10x EPS targets while the company forecasts at least 60% sales growth in the upcoming fiscal year.

data.title: Denny's: A Forgotten Stock With A Hidden Growth Driver
data.description:
Many readers are likely familiar with Denny's (NASDAQ:DENN); home of the Original Grand Slam. But with a market cap of just under $250 million and a poor performance over the past decade, many have also likely forgotten about the stock. An underwhelming earnings call last week in which management warned about a cautious consumer pulling back in recent weeks added insult to injury for Denny's shareholders, as the stock pulled back about 18% the day it reported.

However, the old adage says to buy when there's blood in the streets, and many great investment opportunities can be found in the ugliest of situations, so I was intrigued by the selloff and motivated to see what the iconic American company has to offer investors.

I’m bullish on Denny’s based on its inexpensive valuation and its ownership of an attractive asset that may have considerably more growth potential than its nameplate brand.

What is Denny’s?

Denny’s is a family restaurant chain mostly associated with breakfast and perhaps best known for its ‘Grand Slam’ deal. The first Denny’s opened in Southern California in 1954, and the company, which is now based in South Carolina, went public in 1968.

Today, there are 1,499 Denny’s locations around the world, with 1,334 of these in the United States, and all but 61 of these are franchised. Additionally, Denny’s is the owner of the Keke’s Breakfast Café Brand. There are 69 Keke’s locations; 55 of which are franchised.

We'll delve further into Keke's Breakfast Café shortly.

Recent Results

Before specifically touching on Denny's recent earnings results, I'd be remiss if I didn't mention that this stock is down nearly 60% over the past decade, so this isn't an investment for the risk-averse or faint of heart, and it's not simply an opportunity to 'buy the dip' on a long-term compounder. It's more a bet on a deep value play and a turnaround based on the emergence of a hidden growth driver, which we'll discuss in the next section.

The quarter was perhaps underwhelming, but it certainly wasn't terrible. The company reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.14, missing by $0.01. Its revenue of $114.7 million missed by $1.34 million and was about 0.6% lower than last year. On a positive note, the company brought in $22.1 million in adjusted EBITDA, a strong 11.1% increase from last year.

But what really caused the stock to sell off was management's commentary about consumers pulling back in a more cautious environment. Denny's management reported that, "...when we entered 2025, there was a feeling of the consumer stabilizing and a sense of normalcy ahead... Yet, there has been a shift in consumer sentiment and a slowing that persisted through the remainder of January and has seemingly accelerated in the last few weeks given the evolving macroenvironment."

The commentary spooked the market and also caused many other restaurant stocks to fall, so this isn't a Denny's-specific challenge, but it's a challenge, nonetheless.

That said, looking beyond next quarter's challenges, there is quite a bit to like about the stock.

Keke’s Breakfast Café; Denny’s Hidden Jewel

Founded in Florida in 2006, Keke’s is focused on serving fresh, made-to-order breakfast fare. It’s also a hidden jewel within Denny’s portfolio, as it harbors solid growth potential. The company has a strong presence in Florida, and over 65% of Keke’s current locations are in the Orlando and Tampa markets. In all, Keke’s has 69 locations across six states. This means that Keke’s has plenty of room to grow in terms of both store count and geographic expansion.

That’s exactly what Keke’s is doing, opening eight new cafes during the fourth quarter of 2024 and entering four new states for the first time (California, Colorado, Texas, and Nevada). The company also signed new development commitments, bringing its total pipeline to 140, which would more than double its total store count. The company now has what it calls a “rapidly expanding group of franchisees” and smartly plans to leverage its existing network of Denny’s franchisees to further fuel near-term growth.

Keke’s posted positive same store sales growth of 3.0% during the fourth quarter, after several quarters of negative same store sales, indicating that the existing locations have staying power. It's worth noting that management believes same store sales would have been about 1% higher if not for Hurricanes Helene and Milton, as the majority of Keke's locations are in Florida.

Keke’s is in the process of remodeling and revamping older locations to give them a newer, fresher look, completing three remodels during the fourth quarter. Early results of the efforts are promising as they have provided a 6-8% sales boost, a 30% IRR, and roughly 25% cash-on-cash returns.

Earlier this month, Wedbush Securities analyst Nick Setyan upgraded his rating on Denny’s stock from Neutral to Outperform and said that the Keke’s brand is undervalued and underappreciated, and he sees it contributing to overall Denny’s EBITDA growth of 5-7% per year going forward.

Valuation

As you might expect for what is perceived to be a sleepy restaurant chain, shares of Denny’s are cheap, trading at just 13.4 times consensus fiscal 2024 earnings estimates.

With earnings per share expected to increase from $0.53 in fiscal 2024 to $0.59 in fiscal 2025, shares look even cheaper at 11.9 times forward earnings estimates.

With the S&P 500 (SPX) trading at 25.6 times earnings, Denny’s is significantly cheaper than the broader market.

This undemanding valuation should give Denny’s stock real downside protection in the event of a market retraction, and also gives it ample room for upside via multiple expansion if it continues to grow earnings per share and if the market begins to give it more credit for the attractiveness of the Keke’s concept.

Returns to Shareholders

While Denny’s does not pay a dividend, it does return capital to shareholders via share repurchases. In 2024, the company repurchased about $11.2 million worth of shares, which is not an insignificant amount when considering that the company’s market cap is just $XM.

In fact, since the company’s share repurchase program began in 2010, Denny’s has reduced its shares outstanding by nearly 50%.

Denny's says it expects to repurchase between $15 million and $25 million worth of shares in fiscal 2025. For a company with a sub-$250 million market cap, this is no small sum.

A Good Speculative Opportunity

In conclusion, I'll reiterate that this is more of a bottom-fishing opportunity for risk-tolerant investors than an opportunity for risk-averse investors or for dip-buyers to buy shares of a long-term winner for a quick bounce back.

That out of the way, I think there is a lot to like here. The company is profitable, it is extremely cheap, and it is buying back shares. Plus, the growth potential of Keke's Breakfast Café to go from being a regional hit in Florida to breaking out as a nationwide hit is feasible, and this would give the company a real growth driver and the market would likely rerate the stock. As we've seen recently with other restaurant stocks like Chili's parent company Brinker International (EAT), when a restaurant chain returns to growth and regains popularity, shares can re-rate quickly. Brinker is up 260% over the past year as Chili's has become a surprise hit with younger Millennial and Gen-Z consumers.

For these reasons, I believe Denny's is an interesting high-risk, high-reward play that is worthy of a smaller, speculative investment within one's portfolio.

data.title: Rogers Communications: Focusing On Debt Reduction Will Prove Fruitful
data.description:
All financial information is from Rogers Investor Relations or Seeking Alpha, unless otherwise noted.

All figures are in Canadian Dollars unless otherwise noted.

Debt Reduction Will Drive Multiple Expansion

Rogers Communications Inc. (NYSE:RCI)(TSX:RCI.B:CA) share price has fallen by approximately 10% in 2025 while at the same time, the Canadian market (SPTSX) has increased by approximately 3%. Moreover, key competitors over that period including Bell (BCE:CA) and Telus (T:CA) are up around 1.4% and 11.9% respectively. We believe the recent pullback in Rogers' share price is overdone, as the company now trades at the lowest EV/EBITDA multiple among its peers. With the clearest path to debt reduction and a well-defined strategy, we expect Rogers to rebound if management executes effectively.

How Did Rogers Get Here?

Rogers currently has the highest debt/equity ratio of their peers at 458% while Bell is at 223% and Telus is at 178%. These figures may be troubling for some but this isn't unusual in the telecommunication industry. Rogers had a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of 4.5x as of the end of 2024. Comparably, Bell had a ratio of 3.8x and Telus had a ratio of 3.9x. Compared to the other Canadian telcos, Quebecor (QBR.B:CA) and Cogeco (CCA:CA) both had ratios of 3.3x. It's clear to us that Rogers has the highest debt burden, but how did they get here?

On April 3, 2023, Rogers acquired Shaw Communications for approximately $26 billion and was expected to yield synergies of $1 billion within two years. As noted in the Q4 2024 press release, over $1 billion in synergies have been achieved one year ahead of schedule. Rogers already had a debt burden similar to Telus and Bell before the transaction, and the acquisition increased their debt to new levels.

More recently, on September 18, 2024, Rogers announced an agreement with Bell to acquire Bell's 37.5% ownership in Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment "MLSE" for $4.7 billion. If approved, the deal would bring Rogers ownership in MLSE to 75%. MLSE owns several professional sports teams and venues, including the Toronto Maple Leafs, Toronto Raptors, and Toronto FC. MLSE also owns and operates the Scotiabank Arena. We believe this deal makes strategic sense for Rogers, and it was expected from many investors. However, the purchase price was slightly higher than we expected and it's also being completed on an accelerated timeline.

The acquisition of Shaw and the potential acquisition of the 37.5% stake in MLSE has led Rogers to a situation where they are more leveraged than their peers, and we believe this is the main cause for Rogers' underwhelming start to 2025.

2025 Guidance Reinforces a Slower Growth Outlook

Rogers provided investors with guidance for 2025 in the Q4 2024 Investor Presentation which, while offering only modest growth, is in line with our expectations. Please note that these estimates don't include any impact from the pending MLSE deal.

Path to Deleveraging and Rerating Opportunity

On October 24, 2024, Rogers announced a potential $7 billion structured equity investment, with nearly all proceeds earmarked for debt repayment. Under the proposed structure, Rogers would create a new subsidiary holding part of its wireless backhaul infrastructure (the network connecting cell towers to core systems) and sell a minority stake in this entity while retaining operational control.

If the 37.5% MLSE stake acquisition proceeds, net debt would theoretically rise to $48.14 billion. However, if the $7 billion equity investment is completed, net debt would decline to $41.14 billion, reducing the net debt/EBITDA ratio from 4.5x to 4.28x.

We are optimistic about 2025 forecasted free cash flow of $3.1 billion. With 2024 dividends totalling $1.07 billion and the most recent Q4 2024 dividend at $269 million, we estimate 2025 quarterly dividends at $275 million—a payout ratio of approximately 35% of free cash flow. This leaves $2 billion available for debt reduction and reinvestment. While Bell (11.81% forward yield) and Telus (7.38%) offer significantly higher dividends than Rogers (5.03%), Rogers' dividend is the only one well-covered by EPS and free cash flow. We prefer this policy, as it provides greater financial flexibility and prevents situations like Bell’s recent struggles.

Excluding the MLSE deal and proposed structured equity investment, if Rogers were to use $2 billion in free cash flow to reduce debt (while acknowledging this is not fully realistic), net debt/EBITDA would decline to 4.23x, bringing Rogers closer to Bell and Telus. Looking forward, we feel Rogers is also best positioned to continue deleveraging, whereas Bell and Telus are unlikely to see significant reductions.

With management aiming to lower the net debt/EBITDA ratio to 3.5x by April 2026, we expect ongoing synergies from the Shaw acquisition to persist, albeit at a lower rate. Combined with potential asset sales or equity refinancing, we believe Rogers can become more competitive on debt metrics relative to Bell and Telus. If Rogers achieves similar debt levels and risk metrics while maintaining the greatest financial flexibility due to its lower dividend payout ratio, we expect its EV/EBITDA multiple to re-rate above those peers. This isn't a new concept either, Rogers was above both Bell and Telus from around early 2022 to the start of 2024.

Our Conclusion

Rogers' focus on deleveraging with disciplined capital allocation and structural advantages from the Shaw acquisition positions it well to improve its financial standing relative to Bell and Telus. If successful, we expect its EV/EBITDA multiple to re-rate above its peers, returning to levels seen between early 2022 and early 2024.

We believe the combination of price return and dividend return (total return) over the next two years will outperform Bell and Telus, and investors currently have an opportunity to pick up Rogers while it's yielding 5.03% compared to the 5-year average of 3.59%. Moreover, the sustainability of the dividend, in our view, is significantly higher than Bell and Telus.

Risks

The primary risk to our thesis is the unsuccessful execution of Rogers' debt reduction strategy. Our main concern is the potential approval of the MLSE transaction without the structured equity investment materializing. That said, we believe the MLSE transaction presents significant synergy opportunities and aligns with Rogers' long-term strategic vision.

It’s important to note that this is just one of many risks associated with an investment in Rogers. We strongly encourage investors to conduct their own due diligence before making any investment decisions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Riding The Semiconductor Boom: Why ASM International Is Poised For Growth
data.description:
Overview

ASM International NV (OTCQX:ASMIY) is a Europe-based leading semiconductor equipment manufacturer. They specialize in deposition technologies, particularly Atomic Layer Deposition. Deposition is the process of coating a silicon wafer with extremely thin layers of materials in order to build small electronic components inside a semiconductor chip. ASM is one of the companies providing advanced technology tools to chipmakers like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), Intel (INTC), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). It is also a major player in the logic/foundry and the memory space. ASM has focused on expansion through both organic and inorganic methods, focusing on both R&D and acquisitions.

The figure below highlights the semiconductor value chain and point where ASM International operates. The value chain starts off with the raw materials and the silicon wafers, then to semiconductor design and manufacturing, followed by semiconductor equipment (where ASM International operates) to finally assembly, and integration.

In recent times, even with headwinds like geopolitical tensions and cyclicity of the industry, ASM has been performing well with factors like rising demand for semiconductors, silicon carbide business expansion and rising spending by ASM on R&D. It is therefore for these reasons, I believe that despite recent risks like China dependency, geopolitical tensions, that ASM International is currently undervalued and is likely to generate sufficient free cash flows.

Tailwinds

Explosion in Demand for Semiconductor due to AI craze

A report by McKinsey has stated that the semiconductor market is expected to become a trillion-dollar industry by 2030. The figure below displays the continuing and the expected growth rate of the semiconductor industry for the upcoming few years till 2030.

A large part of the growth is expected to be driven by computing and data storage, wireless communication, and automotive electronics, which is expected to display the most impressive growth rate of 13% CAGR.

As we have previously mentioned, ASM International is a major player in the logic/foundry and the memory segment. The vertical in which these are used the most is the computing and data storage. Looking from a long-term perspective, I feel that this is one of the safest verticals for companies operating in the semiconductor space because the demand for computing and data storage will persist as we gather more data to train and process them. Moreover, ASM’s equipment is increasingly coming to use in the vertical of automotive electronics, which already has and is also expected to display impressive growth at a CAGR of 13%.

According to a recent press release on 2024 Q3 results, the book-to-bill ratio has mostly remained stable historically at 1.0 but for 2024, it is again expected to increase to around 1.1. New orders have been mostly from the foundry segment, followed by the memory segment and then by the logic segment. With increasing trends towards AI integration into smartphones, the demand for foundry services like that of TSMC is expected to rise. Similarly for the memory segment, according to a report by PwC, we expect the demand for HBM (high-bandwidth memory) to rise from the period of 2023 to 2028 at a CAGR of 21.67% while the overall memory segment is expected to display a CAGR of 12.81% from the period of 2023 to 2030.

Therefore, with the positive outlook for the top two segments and the overall optimism regarding the AI growth, I expect the sales of ASM International to increase which is likely to translate to higher free cash flow, directly impacting shareholder value by raising the intrinsic value of the company.

Silicon Carbide Epi Business Expansion

ASM International has recently introduced the PE2O8, which is a new system aimed towards enhancing the production of silicon carbide (SiC) wafers. SiC wafers are essential components for high power electronic devices, namely, electric vehicles (EVs) and solar power. Based on the current industry trends, these two industries are becoming increasingly popular. The figure below shows the sales curve for EVs in the world and some of the major markets for EVs.

The graph shows that the demand and sales share of EVs and the path being followed displays a hockey-stick pattern that is likely to take off. Looking at forward-looking estimates, I believe that the demand for EVs will sustain due to price decreases. According to a report by Goldman Sachs, EV battery prices in 2025 will be half of the level of 2019 prices.

The reasons as to why SiC wafers are likely to be used heavily are numerous, like enhanced thermal performance, which helps in dissipating heat more effectively; greater electron mobility which allows for higher switching speed and improved power conversion efficiency offering higher voltage breakdown systems.

The demand for solar power is also likely to follow an upward trend. The graph below shows the global energy production from solar power. It has shown an increase in the production levels and is expected to continue displaying the same as well.

So, both of the segments which are consumers of silicon carbide wafers are likely to keep growing rapidly. Moreover, since the semiconductor industry is prone to the bullwhip effect, the growth at the end of the value chain is likely to percolate to the upper parts of the value chain as well.

Therefore, based on the optimism pertaining to these two segments, I believe that the demand for silicon carbide wafers is also likely to remain high, which provides a solid justification for ASM’s recent foray into the production of the same by introducing PE2O8 as well. I believe that the investment is likely to pay off in terms of handsome revenue growth for ASMIY.

Financials

In order to understand the financial health of ASM International, we have looked at the profitability, liquidity & solvency margins and the cash flows and how these ratios convey the nature of the business of ASM and how sustainable is the business model.

The figure above shows that revenues have been increasing consistently since 2019, displaying an impressive CAGR of 16.51%. The profit margins have displayed cyclicity that is characteristic of the semiconductor industry. The NPM in particular displays significant cyclicity, fluctuating in the range of 16% (in 2022) and 29% (in 2021 and 2023). The reason for the same has been changes in the net investment income and changes in irregular expenses like write-off/write-downs of assets.

When talking about the liquidity and solvency ratios, a clear trend that arises is that the liquidity ratios – cash ratio, quick ratio and current ratio – have dropped significantly from 2019 (0.87%) to 2023 (0.70%). The ratio used for measuring solvency ratio is the debt-to-equity ratio. The D/E ratio came down significantly in 2020 and has mostly remained stable since then. This has been mostly because of retirement of previously existing debt and the R&D expenditure that ASM has been undertaking.

The cause of the reductions in the liquidity and solvency ratios has already been discussed. However, moving forward, I will look at the cash flow structure – the sources and the destination of cash flow. The following waterfall structure describes the cash flow values from each of the different categories – operating, investing and financing. A major portion of the investing cash flow goes into capitalizing the R&D spending. A major portion of the debt outstanding and also payment for outstanding common stock. Therefore, ASM manages to recuperate only a small portion of the operating cash flows as free cash flows.

In terms of forward-looking estimates, I expect revenues to increase due to the favoring tailwinds for ASM. However, there are likely to be some short-term headwinds as well, like an increase in SG&A expenses which is likely to shrink the profit margins, directly impacting the operating cash flows.

So, the financials portray a positive picture where ASM is clearly having stable profit margins, rising revenues and therefore, healthy operating cash flows. With these healthy cash flows, ASM is able to undertake investment projects and R&D and at the same time repay debt and common stock payments and at the end still have free cash flows.

Valuation

Having analyzed the relevant tailwinds, I have also done a DCF valuation to estimate the intrinsic value of ASM International. I have assumed a 10-year horizon where I expect their revenue to grow at a rate of 15% approximately as they themselves declared in their earnings call. However, they also mentioned drying up of sales in the year of 2025 from China due to increased regulations in the short term. I have also assumed a terminal growth rate of 3.5% because many countries like India are also investing in building their semiconductor capabilities and that is where ASM International has significant opportunities to explore as well. Moreover, it operates in an industry that is likely to witness significant growth due to the increasing demand for technology.

The beta value was calculated based on a longer time horizon as well. Therefore, the intrinsic valuation that we get is $624.23 per share equity value, which gives us an upside of 3.96%. Even though the margin of upside that we are getting is minimal, I believe that it is currently impacted by the negative outlook. In the longer run, however, we are likely to expect significant growth for ASM International. Moreover, ASM International is currently financed largely by equity (99.42%) and I expect that with time ASM International is likely to take on more debt once their business approaches a more mature stage which will reduce their cost of capital.

Headwinds

Dependency on China

ASM’s majority revenue comes from the Asian region, as highlighted by the graph below (in millions of euros). Even among Asia, most of it comes from China. This highlights the high dependency that ASM has on the manufacturing giant. This can possibly come at a risk, both politically and economically.

When looking at it from an economic point of view, ASM is exposing itself to a country which is experiencing a slowdown in growth. Moreover, with the China + 1 strategy gaining popularity, the demand for Chinese chips and semiconductors are also likely to fall in the coming times. Looking at it from a political angle, note that the Trump 2.0 administration has slapped tariffs on Chinese products. Therefore, the reduced demand for Chinese chips could result in a negative bullwhip effect on ASM’s semiconductor equipment.

Moreover, according to ASM’s annual report, the demand for semiconductor equipment in China is also expected to normalize after a sudden spurt of growth in 2023. Therefore, there is also likely to be short-term headwinds in China as well for ASM International.

Conclusion

The semiconductor industry is one which is currently witnessing an arms race between the giants of semiconductor and equipment manufacturers. Therefore, it is crucial to innovate in this field. The alternate option in the short run is also to follow the inorganic path and acquire different companies in order to gain access to resources like technological knowhow.

ASM operates in the same industry but at an upper level in the value chain where it is responsible for manufacturing semiconductor equipment which is then subsequently sold to chip manufacturers like TSM, Samsung, and Intel. Due to this, any changes at the end market acts as a bullwhip effect on the business of ASM. Currently, the business decisions of ASM have been such that they are likely to pay off for them given the market conditions, like increasing demand for semiconductors, especially for foundry and memory services, and rise in the solar power and the EV segments which will lead to rise in the demand for silicon carbide wafers. Even though there are headwinds like geopolitical risks and China dependency, the tailwinds are strong enough to warrant a positive outlook for the business.
